Cyprien Tanguay’s Genealogical Dictionary
paragraphs: Note:  Many of the early French fur trappers and traders took native women as wives/partners.  A number had two families, one in the French settlements where the union was blessed by the Catholic church and their children were duly baptized and another second native family living in and around the French trading posts and forts around of the Great Lakes.  The children of all mixed French/Native families were known as “Métis children”.

title: Life in the Canada Colony
paragraphs: In 1621, Robert Giffard, a gentleman-surgeon and apothecary, made his first trip to the Quebec settlement Canada.  Robert Giffard, as a naval surgeon (a ship’s doctor responsible for the health of the people about the ship) who was living in Tourouvre, Perche, France at the time.

Note:  Early before daylight on 6 June 1944, during the Second World War, the Pegasus Bridge was taken from the Germans by a small elite glider-borne American force of 6th Airborne Division.  Once captured, the bridge was held against repeated attacks by a much larger German force, preventing the Germans from launching a large counterattack on the Allied troops on the Normandy beaches.

In 1624, the Dutch established a permanent settlement of New Netherland, (present-day New York City).  They quickly set up an extensive fur trade operation out of Fort Orange (present-day Albany, New York).  The Dutch next moved into present-day Connecticut.  Tensions gradually increased between the Dutch and the Algonquian Native people in the area.  The Dutch settlers’ cattle and hogs got lose and invaded the Algonquian villages’ cornfields.  The Algonquian retaliated by killing the invading animals.  A series of wars followed between the Dutch settlers and the Algonquian natives.

In 1626, the Native American Iroquois Confederation destroyed the Mohicans tribe and became the dominate native force in eastern North America south of the St. Lawrence River.  They next set their sights on the land north of the St. Lawrence River.

title: Return to Québec
paragraphs: On 22 Mar 1633, Samuel de Champlain returned to the Québec settlement and began construction of a new settlement based on the framework of a traditional French “ville” (town) that was common in France at the time.  The Québec settlement’s population was split into two groups.  The first group were the colonial elites, including clergy, government officials, skilled craftsmen who lived in the upper town.  The second group were the merchants, sailors and craftsmen that live in the lower town down on the river edge.  The old small settlement of Québec soon began to grow into a real town.

title: Must have either signed a marriage contract to get married in the Canada colony.
paragraphs: Note:  From the beginning of the Québec settlement, tensions existed between the Roman Catholics and the Protestant Huguenots settlers in Canada.  Back in France the Huguenots were generally better off financially and were hated for their economic successes.  The seaports of Dieppe and La Rochelle were Huguenot strongholds.  The protestant seafarers from these French ports enjoyed a life of freedom and independence not available to the rest of the French Catholic people.

title: Île d’Orléans Island
paragraphs: Note:  In 1663, the establishment of a Conseil Souverain, officially restructured the Canada colony and turned the colony into a Royal Province of France ended the Company of the “One Hundred-Associates” involvement in Canada.  From this time on Canada was control by a Governor appointed by the King of France, who was the ceremonial and military head of the French colony.

title: Filles du Roi or “King’s Daughters”
paragraphs: By 1663, the Québec settlement had grown from the small settlement of thirty homes back in 1650 to become small city in thirteen-years with over one hundred homes and over 500 residents.

title: Start of the Hudson’s Bay Company
paragraphs: Note: By 1681, Québec had grown into a city with over 1,300 people and a census was taken.

title: King William’s War
paragraphs: In 1698, French colonists begin to settle east of the Beauport River on the North side of the St. Lawrence River, attracted by a flour mill and good agriculture land.

Note:  In 1700, around 10,000 people of French origin were living and farming in the lower St. Lawrence River valley in Canada.  By comparison over 250,000 people were living in the English American colonies at the same time.

title: Dakota Territory
paragraphs: Note:  In 1867, the “British North American Act” created the Dominion of Canada, a confederation of Ontario, Québec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick under the Crown of the United Kingdom of Great Britain.
